q: What is the strongest single reason to reach for a NoSQL store like Cassandra/DynamoDB over Postgres?
options: Very high write throughput with known key-based access patterns
why: The real differentiator is the access pattern + write profile — not ACID. NoSQL shines for huge write throughput and queries by a known key; SQL wins for complex/ad-hoc queries, joins, and transactions.

q: You're designing a billing/ledger system at massive scale. Which is the better default and why?
options: SQL — you need multi-row transactions, integrity and ad-hoc queries even if it costs scale
Either, it doesn't matter at scale
why: Finance/billing is the classic 'SQL despite scale' case: transactions, joins and auditability matter more than raw write throughput. NoSQL fits activity feeds, telemetry, chat — not ledgers.

q: Which workloads are natural fits for NoSQL? (select all)
why: Feeds, telemetry and chat are high-volume, key-accessed, tolerant of eventual consistency. Double-entry accounting needs transactions → SQL.

q: What problem does sharding primarily solve?
options: Horizontal scaling beyond one node's capacity
why: Sharding = horizontal partitioning to scale past a single node. It does NOT remove replication, and it can actually create hotspots.

q: Which are genuine pains introduced by sharding? (select all)
why: The three big pains: hotspots, cross-shard joins/transactions, and the cost of resharding. Indexes still exist — just per shard.

q: You shard by user_id with a hash. A few celebrity users overwhelm their shard. Best first mitigation?
options: Switch the whole DB to range sharding
Add a secondary cache / split the hot key (sub-sharding, dedicated handling)
why: Hotspots are handled by isolating/splitting the hot key (dedicated shard, key-splitting, caching the celebrity), not by re-architecting the whole scheme.

q: What defines MULTI-leader replication (vs single-leader)?
options: A standby that takes over on failover
Several nodes accept writes simultaneously (e.g. multi-DC)
One node serves reads, another serves writes
Clients write to a quorum of nodes
why: Multi-leader = multiple nodes accept writes at once (common across data centers); its core pain is write-conflict resolution. It is NOT 'failover' — that's single-leader.

q: In leaderless replication (Dynamo/Cassandra), how is consistency tuned?
options: By electing a leader per partition
By quorum: ensure W + R > N
why: Leaderless: client writes/reads multiple replicas; you get strong-ish consistency when write quorum + read quorum exceed the replica count (W + R > N).

q: 'Read-after-write consistency' guarantees that…
options: The system waits for full replication before any read
A user immediately sees their OWN write
All users see the write at the same time
why: Read-your-writes = a user sees their own just-made write, even if other replicas lag. It does NOT mean global sync or that everyone sees it instantly.

q: Single-leader replication handles a leader crash via…
options: Leader election / failover to a replica
why: Single-leader: all writes go to one node; on crash a replica is promoted via failover/leader election.

q: What do the three letters in CAP stand for?
why: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance. (Easy to blank on A & P under pressure — anchor it.)

q: The 'real' CAP choice in practice is between…
options: Consistency vs Availability DURING a partition
why: Partitions are inevitable, so you can't trade away P. The actual decision is C vs A when a partition happens: CP refuses to answer; AP answers possibly-stale.

q: What does PACELC add beyond CAP?
options: Even with no partition (Else), you trade Latency vs Consistency
A way to avoid partitions entirely
why: PACELC: if Partition → choose A or C; Else (normal operation) → choose Latency or Consistency. It captures the everyday latency cost of strong consistency.

q: In cache-aside, who writes to the cache on a miss?
options: The application code, after reading from the DB
why: Cache-aside (lazy loading): app checks cache, on miss reads DB and then populates the cache. Write-through writes cache+DB synchronously; write-back defers the DB write.

q: A popular key expires and thousands of requests hit the DB at once to rebuild it. This is…
why: Cache stampede / thundering herd. Mitigate with a lock/single-flight (one rebuild), TTL jitter, or refresh-ahead.

q: Which mitigate a cache stampede? (select all)
options: Single-flight / lock so only one request rebuilds
Add TTL jitter so keys don't expire together
why: Lock/single-flight, TTL jitter and refresh-ahead all spread or collapse the rebuild. Disabling the cache just sends everything to the DB.

q: Beyond eviction, the two hard cache problems are staleness/invalidation and…
why: Invalidation/staleness and stampede are the two that bite in interviews.

q: You store user-uploaded videos. What goes in the relational DB?
options: The raw binary as a BLOB column
A base64 string of the file
why: Keep binaries in object storage (S3/GCS) and store only the URL/reference in the DB — binaries wreck index and cache efficiency.

q: A CDN's main job is to…
options: Run application logic at the edge
Geo-cache static content near users, cutting latency and offloading origin
why: CDN = geographically distributed edge cache for static assets; reduces latency and origin load. It often sits in front of blob storage.

q: Redis command execution model is…
options: Single-threaded — commands run atomically, but a heavy command blocks the whole box
why: Core architectural fact: Redis executes commands single-threaded. That gives atomicity for free, but an expensive command (e.g. KEYS, big sort) stalls everything.

q: A single-node Redis is best described as…
why: Single node is consistent (one copy, atomic ops) but not durable by default (in-memory). Common miss: saying it 'sacrifices consistency' — it sacrifices durability.

q: How does Redis Cluster map keys to nodes?
options: 16384 fixed hash slots, slot = CRC16(key) mod 16384
why: Redis Cluster uses 16384 fixed hash slots (CRC16 mod 16384), NOT consistent hashing. Slots move between nodes on rebalance; clients follow MOVED/ASK redirects.

q: What is the DEFAULT eviction policy in Redis?
options: noeviction (writes error when memory is full)
why: Default is noeviction — once maxmemory is hit, writes fail rather than silently dropping data. You must opt into LRU/LFU for a pure cache.

q: Which give Redis durability options? (select all)
why: RDB (point-in-time snapshots) + AOF (logs every write; everysec ≈ 1s loss, always = no loss but slow). For true durability use MemoryDB. Single-threading is about execution, not durability.

q: How do you make a multi-step Redis operation (e.g. a rate-limiter check-and-decrement) atomic?
options: Wrap it in a Lua script (or MULTI/EXEC)
Use a transaction across two nodes
Rely on single-threading of separate commands
Add a mutex in the client
why: A Lua script runs atomically on the server; MULTI/EXEC(+WATCH) also works. Separate commands aren't atomic relative to each other even though each one is.

q: Redis Pub/Sub vs Redis Streams — the key difference?
options: Pub/Sub is at-most-once (no persistence); Streams persist messages with consumer groups
why: Pub/Sub = fire-and-forget, at-most-once (offline subscribers miss messages). Streams persist the log and support consumer groups + replay — closer to Kafka-lite.

q: Redlock (distributed lock across Redis nodes) — what's the nuance a senior should mention?
options: Kleppmann's critique: it's unsafe under GC pauses/clock skew — use fencing tokens / a real consensus system for correctness-critical locks
It only works on one node
why: Redlock works for efficiency locks but Kleppmann showed it's not safe for correctness under pauses/clock issues. Mention fencing tokens or a consensus store (ZK/etcd) for hard locks.

q: Key difference between an L4 and an L7 load balancer?
options: L4 routes by IP/port (content-blind); L7 sees HTTP URL/headers/cookies → content routing, SSL termination, sticky sessions
why: L4 = transport (TCP/UDP), fast but content-blind. L7 = application layer, can route by URL/header/cookie, terminate TLS, and do sticky sessions.

q: Which are valid load-balancing algorithms? (select all)
why: Round-robin, least-connections, weighted, consistent hashing are LB strategies. 2PC is a distributed-transaction protocol.

q: The defining property of Kafka vs a traditional broker queue (SQS/RabbitMQ)?
options: Kafka is an append-only log: messages are retained & replayable; consumers track their own offset
why: Kafka = distributed append-only log. Messages are retained (replayable), consumers track offsets, multiple consumer groups read the same data independently, ordered within a partition.

q: In SQS/RabbitMQ, what happens to a message after a consumer acks it?
why: Broker queues delete on ack — typically one consumer, no replay. Good for simple background tasks and decoupling, not stream replay/analytics.

q: Why put a message queue between services at all? (select all)
why: Queues decouple, smooth/buffer bursts, and throttle downstream. They don't provide strong consistency — they generally push you toward eventual consistency.

q: You need 3 independent teams to each process the same event stream their own way. Best fit?
options: Kafka with separate consumer groups per team
A database table they poll and delete from
why: Kafka's multiple consumer groups each maintain their own offset over the same retained log — exactly the multi-consumer fan-out case.

q: Fan-out-on-write (push) for a social timeline means…
options: Build each timeline at read time
On post, write the item into every follower's precomputed timeline
Pull tweets from followees when the user opens the app
Cache the home page in a CDN
why: Push = on write, fan the post into every follower's feed. Fast reads, expensive writes; it breaks for celebrities with millions of followers.

q: Why is pure fan-out-on-write a disaster for a celebrity with 100M followers?
options: A single post triggers 100M timeline writes
why: One celebrity post = 100M writes. That's why the standard answer is hybrid: push for normal users, pull (fan-out-on-read) for celebrities.

q: The standard production answer for a Twitter-style timeline is…
options: Hybrid: push for normal users, pull for celebrities
why: Hybrid balances write cost and read latency: precompute for the many, compute-on-read for the few huge accounts.

q: How does the token-bucket algorithm work?
options: Counts requests in fixed windows and resets
Holds N tokens, refills at a fixed rate; each request takes a token; empty → reject
Delays every request by a fixed amount
why: Token bucket: bucket of N tokens, refilled at a steady rate; a request consumes a token, empty bucket rejects. Allows bursts up to bucket size.

q: Where should a rate limiter usually live?
options: At the edge — API gateway / load balancer — per user/IP/API-key
why: Put it at the edge (gateway/LB) so abuse is rejected before it reaches downstream services; key it per user/IP/API-key for fairness and DDoS protection.

q: Which limiter property does token bucket give that a fixed-window counter struggles with?
options: Smooth handling of short bursts up to the bucket size
why: Token bucket allows controlled bursts and avoids the fixed-window edge problem (double traffic at the boundary). Sliding-window log/counter also fixes the edge issue.

q: An idempotent operation is one where…
options: It can only run once ever
Applying it many times yields the same result as applying it once
why: Idempotent = repeating it has the same effect as doing it once. Crucial because retries are inevitable (a timeout means the client doesn't know if it succeeded).

q: A payment API must avoid double-charging on client retries. Standard mechanism?
options: Idempotency key supplied by client; server dedupes on it
why: Client sends a unique idempotency key; the server records it and returns the original result on retries instead of re-charging.

q: Where is idempotency essential? (select all)
options: Payment / write APIs that clients retry
why: Retryable writes, at-least-once consumers, and anything over flaky networks need it. Pure GETs are already naturally idempotent.

q: The core problem distributed transactions solve is preserving which ACID property across nodes?
why: It's primarily Atomicity across nodes — all participants commit or none do. (Common miss: saying 'A & I'.) Saga in particular drops isolation.

q: Two-phase commit (2PC) is best characterized as…
options: Pessimistic: prepare+commit phases, holds locks, CP, blocking with a coordinator SPOF
why: 2PC = pessimistic, coordinator-driven prepare/commit, holds locks, CP and blocking; the coordinator is a SPOF (mitigated but real). Used by Spanner/XA.
